mappings.rationaleAI: NATO's role in British foreign policy and the European Economic Community (A.1.a.iv), and German membership in NATO (B.2.a.ii) are directly relevant.
Stalin's rise after Lenin's death, purges and Stalinism (B.1.a.i–ii), WWII Eastern Front and Battle of Stalingrad (B.1.a.iii), and post-war monopoly of power and Cold War relations (B.1.b.i).

Poland's Second Republic, the Polish Corridor, Russo-Polish War, German invasion (B.2.d.i), post-war communist rule and Solidarity (B.2.d.ii), and democratic reform and EU/NATO membership (B.2.d.iii).

Hitler's rise to power, the Third Reich, persecution of Jews, rearmament, and WWII conquests throughout Europe until Allied defeat directly cover Germany from the Weimar Republic through WWII (A.3.a.ii).
WWI's aftermath — economic depression, labour unrest, and Treaty of Versailles reparations — shaped the interwar period in Britain (A.1.a.i) and other European states.

Russia's history from the 1917 Revolution through the Soviet Union to the post-1991 Russian Federation comprehensively covers the Soviet state from establishment through WWII (B.1.a), postwar period (B.1.b), and Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus since 1991 (B.1.c).

The Soviet Union from establishment through Stalin, Khrushchev, Brezhnev, and Gorbachev, including industrialization, WWII, Cold War, glasnost, perestroika, and dissolution covers the entire Soviet trajectory (B.1.a–b).
US Cold War relations with the Soviet Union and other countries are directly covered (B.1.b.i), along with the deterioration of superpower relations.

WWII covers Churchill's Britain standing against the Axis (A.1.a.iii), French defeat, Vichy, and Resistance (A.2.a.iii), the Third Reich's WWII (A.3.a.ii), Mussolini's participation (A.4.a.i), and the German offensive and Soviet advance (B.1.a.iii).
Soviet postwar recovery and Cold War relations (B.1.b.i), de-Stalinization and the Khrushchev era (B.1.b.ii), and Gorbachev's reforms and Soviet dissolution (B.1.b.v) are directly covered.
British colonial and Commonwealth relations, the Indian problem (A.1.a.ii), and disintegration of the British Empire (A.1.a.iv) are directly covered.

Spain's Primo de Rivera military government, the republic, Civil War, Franco's regime (A.5.a.i), postwar rapprochement with NATO (A.5.a.ii), and democratic transition under King Juan Carlos (A.5.a.iii).
France's interwar financial crises, WWII defeat and Resistance (A.2.a), the Fourth Republic and colonial wars, and the Fifth Republic under de Gaulle and successors (A.2.b).
The UK's interwar depression, WWII, post-war welfare state, NATO role, Thatcher's denationalization, devolution, and Brexit comprehensively cover Great Britain since 1920 (A.1.a).
Europe's broad history covering two World Wars, Cold War division, and post-1989 integration maps to the entire section on western (A) and eastern/central (B) European states since 1920.
Germany's Weimar Republic, Third Reich (A.3.a), post-war partition, Federal Republic, Democratic Republic, Berlin Wall, and reunification (A.3.b) are comprehensively covered.
Italy's Fascist era under Mussolini (A.4.a) and postwar republican democracy including the De Gasperi era, ministerial instability, and terrorism (A.4.b).
Italy's conquest of Ethiopia and its connection to Mussolini's rapprochement with Germany are covered under the Fascist era (A.4.a.i).
European history's modern era covering two world wars, the Cold War, and post-1945 economic integration maps to the entire section on western (A) and eastern/central (B) Europe since 1920.
Argentina's Falklands war with Britain (1982) is directly covered under British Conservative government under Thatcher (A.1.a.iv).
Gandhi's independence movement is part of the 'Indian problem' within British colonial and Commonwealth relations in the interwar period (A.1.a.ii).
Disintegration of the British Empire (A.1.a.iv) and French colonial independence movements including the Indochina and Algerian Wars (A.2.b.i) connect to decolonization.
